common_name: Red Tip Photinia (Fraser Photinia, Red Robin Photinia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; full sun is essential for Red Tip Photinia — shade dramatically increases susceptibility to Entomosporium leaf spot, the primary disease threat, and reduces the brilliance of the red new growth that is the plant's main ornamental attribute.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun with good air circulation; Red Tip Photinia is one of the most widely planted and most recognizable landscape shrubs in the American South — a large, fast-growing evergreen producing stunning flushes of brilliant scarlet-red new growth throughout spring and into summer, creating a spectacular multi-season display as the vivid red leaves mature to glossy dark green. This red new growth is the plant's defining ornamental feature and the reason for its extraordinary popularity. Flat-topped clusters of creamy-white flowers appear in spring. Red Tip Photinia grows vigorously to 10–15 feet tall but responds readily to shearing as a formal hedge at whatever height is needed. However, heavy shearing stimulates rapid new growth — which, while ornamentally attractive for its color, also provides dense, susceptible tissue for the plant's primary disease problem: Entomosporium maculatum leaf spot, which causes spreading purple-red spots with gray centers that disfigure and eventually defoliate plants in humid, warm climates. In zone 9, Entomosporium can be severe in dense plantings with poor air circulation. Planting in full sun with wide spacing and avoiding overhead irrigation reduces, but does not eliminate, the risk. The disease has significantly reduced Red Tip Photinia's use in zone 9 plantings since the 1990s; Ternstroemia gymnanthera is sometimes recommended as a disease-resistant alternative providing similar red new growth.

common_name: Japanese Privet (Wax Leaf Privet, Wax Ligustrum)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in nearly any well-drained soil — Japanese privet is one of the most soil-adaptable landscape shrubs in zone 9, tolerating clay, sand, slightly alkaline, and compacted soils that stress most other plants. Full sun to partial shade are both acceptable.

directions: Plant in nearly any well-drained soil — Japanese privet is extraordinary in its adaptability, accepting clay, sand, alkaline soils, compacted urban soils, reflected heat, and neglect without significant decline — in full sun to partial shade; Japanese privet is the most widely planted hedge and screen shrub across the Gulf South, a vigorous, large-growing evergreen producing dense, glossy, waxy, dark-green leaves on stout stems that respond readily to shearing into formal hedges or can be allowed to grow into substantial multi-trunked small trees reaching 18 feet. White flower clusters in late spring have a distinctive, heavy fragrance that some find pleasant and others find objectionable; the flowers produce abundant blue-black berries in fall and winter that are consumed and spread by birds — a characteristic that has made ligustrum a serious invasive plant in natural areas across the Southeast and Gulf South. The berries are mildly toxic to humans and should be noted in gardens used by children. Despite its invasive potential, Japanese privet remains the dominant hedge plant in zone 9 residential and commercial landscapes due to its unmatched combination of heat tolerance, drought tolerance, adaptability, and fast growth. Alternatives for non-invasive hedging include Podocarpus macrophyllus, Ternstroemia gymnanthera, and Distylium.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Invasive Species Guidance

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Ternstroemia is one of the few large evergreen shrubs that performs reliably in partial to full shade in zone 9 — a critical attribute where few options exist.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Ternstroemia gymnanthera is a slow-growing, refined evergreen shrub from Japan and China that has been widely sold in the Gulf South nursery trade under the name 'Cleyera' — though it is botanically distinct from true Cleyera japonica. It produces dense, oval, waxy, dark-green leaves (emerging with distinctive bronze to wine-red new growth) on neat, naturally well-shaped plants with excellent ornamental appeal throughout the year. Small, creamy-white, fragrant flowers appear in summer beneath the foliage, followed by orange-red berries. The combination of beautiful new-growth color and outstanding shade tolerance makes it a valuable alternative to Red Tip Photinia in shaded situations where photinia would fail from disease. Ternstroemia grows slowly and requires no pruning to maintain an attractive shape, ultimately reaching 5–8 feet in typical garden conditions. Unlike Photinia, it is essentially free of serious pest and disease problems in zone 9 — an important advantage in Gulf Coast conditions. It is also deer-resistant. The cultivar 'Burnished Gold' has chartreuse-gold variegated leaves; 'Big Bronze' has exceptionally large, deep-bronze new growth. An outstanding but underutilized alternative to photinia, nandina, and ligustrum in partial to full shade settings.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; distylium is exceptional in its tolerance of both full sun and full shade with no significant change in growth quality — a rare characteristic among broadleaved evergreens.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; Distylium racemosum is a Japanese evergreen shrub in the witch hazel family (Hamamelidaceae) that is gaining significant recognition as one of the most adaptable and low-maintenance broadleaved evergreens for zone 7–10 landscapes. It produces glossy, leathery, dark-green leaves on spreading, arching branches and bears unusual, petal-less winter flowers with prominent red stamens that emerge in February–March in zone 9, providing early-season color before most shrubs bloom. The strongly adaptable nature of distylium is its defining quality — it thrives in full sun to dense shade, in dry or moist soils, in clay or sandy soils, and in both acid and moderately alkaline pH ranges. This adaptability makes it invaluable for difficult landscape situations where few plants perform reliably. Named compact cultivars have made distylium increasingly available in the trade: 'Cinnamon Girl' (4–5 feet, copper-bronze new growth), 'Vintage Jade' (4 feet, spreading), and 'Coppertone' (2.5–3 feet, compact) are among the most widely sold selections. Distylium is completely free of serious pest and disease problems in zone 9, requires no deadheading or shaping, and is highly deer-resistant — a compelling combination for the low-maintenance Gulf South garden.

common_name: Dwarf Creeping Gardenia (Miniature Gardenia, Radicans Gardenia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Radicans is more shade-tolerant than standard gardenias and performs well with 3–4 hours of morning sun followed by afternoon shade, avoiding the leaf scorch that can occur in intense afternoon sun.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Radicans is a low-growing, spreading dwarf form of the common gardenia that stays 12–18 inches tall while spreading 3–4 feet wide, creating a compact, flowing ground cover or low shrub form with all the fragrance and ornamental qualities of its larger relatives in a much more manageable size. The small, double, white flowers (about 1 inch across) are produced abundantly in late spring and summer and carry the same intensely sweet, classic gardenia fragrance as the standard-size plants, making this one of the most fragrant plants available for a low-growing ground cover position. The glossy, dark-green, narrow foliage is evergreen in zone 9 and attractive year-round. Radicans is effective as a ground cover under trees with partial shade, along shaded walkway edges, cascading over retaining walls, and in containers where its low, spreading habit can be displayed. It requires the same acidic, humus-rich, moist soil that all gardenias prefer and is sensitive to soil pH above 6.5 — yellowing foliage (chlorosis) in zone 9 is often a sign of pH too high or iron deficiency in alkaline soils. Excellent for zone 9 where the mild winters allow it to thrive and bloom reliably year after year.
